text: The surgeon removes the gastroesophageal fat pad, a small fatty tissue located at the upper part of the stomach, to enhance the view of the junction between the stomach and esophagus.
The surgeon uses an energy device in the right hand to cauterize the small blood vessels connecting the gastroesophageal fat pad to the stomach at the gastroesophageal junction.
The surgeon has completely transected a small piece of fat at the gastroesophageal junction and is removing it, providing clear access to the angle of His for creating a sleeve gastrectomy.
The surgeon uses a grasper to measure a section of the lower stomach, identifying the pylorus and marking an area four to six centimeters proximal along the greater curvature.

The assistant elevates the stomach as the surgeon begins transecting the short gastric vessels using an energy device, progressing towards the angle of His. The transection continues to join the upper part of the stomach where the gastroesophageal fat pad was previously removed.
The surgeon completes the transection of the short gastric vessels and passes a bougie, a sizing tube, into the stomach to assist in shaping the gastric sleeve.

The surgeon uses a stapling device to transect the stomach alongside a fifty French bougie, creating a larger sleeve as part of the duodenal switch procedure. The final staple completes the sleeve portion of the surgery.
The surgeon performs an inverting over-sewing of the staple line on the sleeve gastrectomy, aiming to completely invert the staples so they are not visible on the outside of the stomach.
The surgeon completes the oversewing of the sleeve gastrectomy portion of the duodenal switch, ensuring the staple line is inverted and not visible on the outside of the stomach.
The fifty French bougie is positioned inside the gastric sleeve, providing a visual guide for the sleeve's tightness. The surgeon prepares to proceed with the duodenal transection.
The camera is directed towards the duodenum as the surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to create a window under and above the duodenum, carefully controlling small blood vessels during the process.

The surgeon transects the remaining vessels around the duodenum, ensuring a clear tunnel is created. A blunt device is then passed posterior to the duodenum, followed by transection using a stapling device.

The stapler is positioned at the duodenum, and after confirming the absence of other structures, the surgeon proceeds to transect the duodenum, completing the duodenal transection portion of the duodenal switch procedure.
The surgeon completes the duodenal transection and proceeds to measure the bowel using a ruler, indicating a length of ten centimeters.

The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz and measures approximately 100 centimeters of bowel in a stair-step fashion, leaving most of the bowel in the right lower quadrant. Clips are placed at the 100-centimeter mark to designate the site for the lower anastomosis.
The surgeon measures and identifies the alimentary limb at 250 centimeters, rotating this loop of bowel into the upper abdomen for the anastomosis in the duodenal switch procedure.

The surgeon rotates the loop of small bowel at the 250-centimeter mark towards the upper abdomen and begins sewing it to the duodenal staple line. Using an endo-stitch device with an absorbable suture, the posterior layer of the two-layered anastomosis is created in a running fashion, attaching the bowel loop to the duodenum across the staple line and ilium.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to create wide openings in the duodenum and ileum, ensuring they are large enough to prevent post-procedure strictures. Following this, the inner layer of the anastomosis is constructed using a running technique with absorbable suture, securing the back wall of the openings.

The surgeon completes the anterior inner layer of the anastomosis using a running suture technique, carefully ensuring the needle passes through only the single layer without catching the back wall, to maintain the opening.

The surgeon completes the anterior inner layer of the anastomosis by tying it to the posterior inner layer, ensuring the inner layer is secure. Following this, the surgeon begins the final anterior outer layer using an inverting suture technique in a running fashion, concluding the two-layered hand-sewn anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a stapling device to transect the biliopancreatic limb, a step in completing the full duodenal switch procedure.

The surgeon disconnects the bile and pancreatic enzymes from the limb of bowel, completing the duodenal ileostomy and transecting the biliopancreatic limb. A flexible endoscope is passed down the esophagus into the antrum, visually confirming the distension of the small bowel without any air leakage, indicating an airtight sleeve gastrectomy and duodenal ileostomy.

The surgeon locates the clips marking the 100-centimeter point on the small bowel and identifies the limb of small bowel for reattachment. An opening is created in the biliopancreatic limb, which is then stapled to the alimentary limb at the marked location, facilitating the joining of digestive enzymes with food for nutrient absorption.

The surgeon uses a double staple technique to create an anastomosis between the two bowel limbs. The first stapler is positioned, and the limbs are stapled next to each other to form an opening. The bowel is rotated to ensure a wide anastomosis without stricture, and the stapler is fired in the opposite direction. The common opening is then closed, completing the lower anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the ilioiliostomy, removing a segment of bowel. The mesenteric defect is then closed in a running suture fashion to prevent herniation of the small bowel through the newly created opening.

The surgeon applies a permanent suture to secure the duodenal ileostomy, ensuring it does not dissolve. Following the completion of the ilioiliostomy and mesenteric closure, a fibrin sealant is used to seal the duodenal ileostomy, marking the final step of the laparoscopic duodenal switch procedure.

The surgeon removes the gastric specimen through a fifteen millimeter port, using a wound protector to facilitate extraction. The liver retractor is then removed, concluding the laparoscopic duodenal switch procedure.
